title: Polymorphism
summary: Learn how the same code can work with many different types of objects — and why that makes your programs flexible and powerful.

blocks.md:
Imagine you have a TV remote. You press the volume-up button and the volume goes up — whether you own a Sony, Samsung, or LG TV. The button is the same. The behavior underneath is different for each brand.

That idea is **polymorphism** (poly = many, morph = form). In Python, you can call the same method name on different objects, and each object responds in its own way. Your code stays clean and simple while handling many different types.

blocks.text: A Simple Example: Animals That Speak

blocks.md: Let's create a few animal classes. Each one has a `speak()` method, but each animal makes a different sound.

blocks.caption: Three classes, same method name, three different results.

blocks.md: Notice that all three classes define a `speak()` method. Python doesn't care which class an object comes from — it just looks for a method called `speak` and calls it. This is polymorphism in action.

blocks.text: Calling Them Uniformly in a Loop

blocks.md: The real power shows up when you put different objects in a list and treat them all the same way.

blocks.caption: One loop, four objects of different types — same call, different behavior.

blocks.title: The Universal Remote Analogy
blocks.md: Think of the `for` loop as a universal remote. It sends the same signal (`speak()`) to every device in the list. Each device — Dog, Cat, Duck — interprets that signal in its own way. You write the loop once, and it works for every animal, even ones you add later.

blocks.text: Duck Typing: If It Quacks Like a Duck...

blocks.md:
Python uses a philosophy called **duck typing**: if an object has the method you need, Python will use it — no questions asked about what *type* it is.

The saying goes: *"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck."* Python doesn't check IDs. It just checks behavior.

blocks.caption: Robot is not an animal, but it has speak() — so the loop works just fine.

blocks.title: You Don't Need a Shared Parent Class
blocks.md: Duck typing means your classes don't have to inherit from the same base class to work together polymorphically. They just need to share the same method **name** and **signature**. This is one of Python's great strengths — it keeps things flexible and simple.

blocks.text: Polymorphism You Already Know: len() and +

blocks.md: You've already been using polymorphism without realizing it! Python's built-in functions like `len()` and the `+` operator work on many different types.

blocks.code:
print(len("hello"))       # works on a string
print(len([1, 2, 3]))     # works on a list
print(len({"a": 1}))     # works on a dictionary

print("cat" + "fish")     # + joins strings
print([1, 2] + [3, 4])   # + joins lists
print(10 + 5)             # + adds numbers
blocks.caption: len() and + behave differently depending on the type — that's polymorphism.

blocks.md: Python calls the appropriate underlying method for each type. When you write `len("hello")`, Python calls the string's internal length method. When you write `len([1, 2, 3])`, it calls the list's internal length method. Same interface, different behavior.
Another form of polymorphism is **method overriding** — when a child class replaces a method it inherited from a parent class.

blocks.caption: Dog and Cat override speak(). Fish inherits the default from Animal.

blocks.md: The parent class `Animal` provides a default `speak()` that returns `"..."`. Dog and Cat **override** it with their own version. Fish doesn't bother, so it falls back to the parent's version. All three still respond to `speak()` — that's polymorphism through method overriding.

blocks.title: Forgetting the Method Name Must Match Exactly
blocks.md:
Polymorphism only works if the method names match **exactly**. If you write `speak()` in one class and `Speak()` or `make_sound()` in another, Python won't treat them as interchangeable — it will raise an `AttributeError` when it can't find the method.

Always double-check your spelling and capitalization when designing a shared interface.

blocks.caption: Circle and Rectangle each compute area differently, but the loop calls area() the same way on both.

blocks.question: Which statement best describes polymorphism in Python?
options: Only objects from the same class can share method names.
Different objects can respond to the same method name in their own unique way.
Polymorphism requires all classes to inherit from a single parent.
You must import a special library to use polymorphism.
blocks.explanation: Polymorphism means different objects can share the same method name and each respond in their own way. In Python, this works even without a shared parent class — thanks to duck typing. As long as an object has the method you're calling, Python will use it.

keyTakeaways: Polymorphism lets you call the same method name on different objects and get behavior appropriate to each.
Duck typing means Python only cares whether an object *has* the method — not what type or class it belongs to.
You can loop over a mixed list of objects and call the same method on all of them — a huge code simplification.
Method overriding is polymorphism: child classes replace parent methods with their own version.
Built-ins like `len()` and `+` are everyday examples of polymorphism you already use.

exercise.prompt: Create three classes: `Trumpet`, `Guitar`, and `Piano`. Each should have a `play()` method that returns a short description of the sound that instrument makes (e.g., `"Trumpet goes: Toot toot!"`). Then create a list containing one of each instrument and loop through it, printing the result of `play()` for each.
exercise.hint: Replace each `pass` with a `return` statement that gives back a fun string describing the sound. Then the loop will handle the rest!
